2.5: heis gar theos heis kai mesites theou kai anthropon anthropos Christos Iesous (heis gar theos, heis kai mesitēs theou kai anthrōpōn, anthrōpos Christos Iēsous): 'For there is one God and one mediator between God and people, the man Christ Jesus.' The mesites (mediator) terminology was used in both Hellenistic contract law (a third party who guarantees a transaction) and in LXX usage for Moses's mediating role at Sinai (Gal 3:19-20). The Christological uniqueness: there is one mediator — contra the Greco-Roman polytheistic model of multiple divine intermediaries, and contra any Jewish or emerging Gnostic scheme of angelic mediators. The qualifier anthrōpos (man) before Christ Jesus emphasizes the genuine humanity that enables the mediation: the mediator must be able to touch both parties.
2.1: parakalō oun prōton pantōn poieisthai deēseis proseukas enteuxeis eucharistias hyper pantōn anthrōpōn — 'I urge, then, first of all, that supplications, prayers, intercessions, and thanksgivings be made for all people.' The four prayer terms represent the full range of petitionary and worshipful address: deēseis (requests, needs-based petitions), proseuchas (prayers generally — the broadest term), enteuxeis (intercessions — interventions on another's behalf, related to entugchanō used of Christ's intercession in Rom 8:34), eucharistias (thanksgivings). The comprehensive scope hyper pantōn anthrōpōn (for all people) is immediately narrowed in v.2 to the practical application: kings and those in authority.
2.2: hyper basileōn kai pantōn tōn en hyperochē ontōn — 'for kings and all who are in high position.' The prayer for rulers is grounded in Jewish Diaspora practice (Jer 29:7 — pray for the welfare of the city; Ezra 6:10; 1 Macc 12:11) and early Christian convention. The goal — hinaēremon kai hēsuchion bion diagōmen (that we may live a quiet and peaceful life) — is social stability rather than political endorsement of specific rulers. Eusebeia kai semnotēs (godliness and dignity) characterize the quality of the life sought: the eusebeia-vocabulary is distinctively Pastoral, appearing 8 times in 1 Timothy alone (out of 15 NT occurrences).
2.3: touto kalon kai apodekton enōpion tou sōtēros hēmōn theou — 'this is good and acceptable before God our Savior.' The divine approval grounds the community practice in the character of God: God's will for universal salvation (v.4) is the reason prayer for all people is fitting. Apodekton (acceptable, pleasing) — a term from the sacrificial vocabulary of things accepted at the altar. The prayer for all people is itself a kind of spiritual offering that pleases the God who wills the salvation of all.
2.4: hos pantas anthrōpous thelei sōthēnai kai eis epignōsin alētheias elthein — 'who desires all people to be saved and to come to a knowledge of the truth.' The universalist scope of pantas anthrōpous (all people) has generated centuries of debate: does it mean every individual without exception, or all types/categories of people? Paul's immediate context (prayer for kings, v.2) suggests he means without distinction of social rank — kings as well as commoners. Epignōsis alētheias (full knowledge of the truth) — epignōsis (precise, experiential knowledge, stronger than gnōsis) is the Pastoral Epistles' characteristic term for saving knowledge (1 Tim 2:4; 2 Tim 2:25; 3:7; Titus 1:1). Truth here is not propositional only but relational.
2.6: ho dous heauton antilutron hyper pantōn — 'who gave himself as a ransom for all.' Antilutron is a Pauline hapax (this is its only NT occurrence) intensifying lutron (ransom, Mark 10:45): the anti prefix marks substitution — a ransom given in place of others. The compound emphasizes the substitutionary nature of Christ's death more explicitly than Jesus's own lutron anti pollōn (Mark 10:45). To marturion kairois idiois (the testimony at its proper times) — the gospel is the divinely appointed disclosure of what Christ's self-giving means, timed by God's sovereignty.
2.7: kērux kai apostolos kai didaskalos ethnōn en pistei kai alētheiā — 'herald, apostle, and teacher of the Gentiles in faith and truth.' Three functional designations: kērux (herald — the proclaimer of news, the one who announces publicly) is the most primitive image of the preacher as town-crier. Apostolos (sent one — with full authorization of the sender). Didaskalos (teacher — the one who explains and instructs). The insistence 'I am telling the truth, I am not lying' (alētheuō, ou pseudomai) is a Pauline oath-formula (cf. Rom 9:1; Gal 1:20) inserted because this particular claim — apostle to the Gentiles — was contested by opponents.
2.8: boulomaioun proseuchesthai tous andras en panti topō epairontas hosias cheiras chōris orgēs kai dialogismou — 'I want the men in every place to pray, lifting holy hands without anger or disputing.' Andras here is male-specific (not anthrōpous = people generally). The posture of prayer — epairontas hosias cheiras (lifting holy hands) — was the standard Jewish and early Christian prayer posture (cf. 1 Kgs 8:22; Ps 28:2; 63:4). Hosias (holy, devout) — the hands must be ritually and morally clean. The qualifiers 'without anger or disputing' suggest that prayer in the Ephesian community was being disrupted by internal conflict — the instruction addresses the social preconditions for legitimate prayer.
2.9: hōsautōs kai gynaikas en katastolē kosmia meta aidous kai sōphrosynēs kosmein heautas — 'likewise women to adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control.' Katastolē (clothing, deportment) covers both dress and general bearing. Aidōs (modesty, a sense of shame before others) and sōphrosynē (self-control, sound-mindedness) were recognized female virtues in both Jewish and Greco-Roman culture. The specific prohibitions — plēgmasin ē chrysiō ē margaritais ē himatismō polytele (braided hair, gold, pearls, expensive clothing) — match similar prohibitions in 1 Pet 3:3 and echo Roman and Jewish moralizing literature about wealthy women's ostentation. The issue is conspicuous wealth-display in worship, not dress codes for their own sake.
2.10: alla ho prepei gynaixi epangellomenais theosebeian di' ergōn agathōn — 'but what is fitting for women who profess godliness — through good deeds.' The contrast: not costly adornment but good deeds is the proper self-presentation for a woman whose claim is theosebeia (reverence toward God, piety — the word is a near-synonym of eusebeia). The verb prepei (is fitting, is appropriate) invokes the category of social propriety — what is congruent with one's stated values. Claiming godliness while displaying wealth is an incongruity; embodying it through good works is consistency.
2.11: gunē en hēsuchia manthanetō en pasē hypotagē — 'a woman is to learn in quietness in all submission.' Hēsuchia (quietness, stillness) is not the same as sigē (silence/speechlessness) — the word connotes a settled, undisruptive disposition rather than absolute silence (cf. its use in 2 Thess 3:12 for 'quietly working'). The command to learn (manthanetō) is itself counter-cultural: many Greco-Roman contexts excluded women from formal instruction. The parallel with 1 Cor 14:34-35 and the qualification en pasē hypotagē (in all submissiveness) — submissive to the teaching authority structure, not to all men universally.
2.12: didaskein de gynaiki ouk epitrepō oude authentein andros alla einai en hēsuchia — 'I do not permit a woman to teach or to exercise authority over a man; she is to remain in quietness.' This is the most contested verse in 1 Timothy. Three issues: (1) epitrepō is present active — a current directive, possibly situation-specific; (2) authentein appears only here in the NT — its semantic range is debated: 'have authority over,' 'domineer,' or even 'instigate' or 'be responsible for'; (3) the scope: all women, or the wives of male leadership? Whether the two infinitives didaskein ... authentein are joined as a hendiadys (teaching-in-an-authoritative-domineering-way) or separately is unresolved. The grounding in creation order (vv. 13-14) has led most interpreters to see the principle as trans-situational, though the situation-specific terms of v.8 ('I want') signal this is directive, not command.
2.13: Adam gar prōtos eplasthē, eita Eua — 'For Adam was formed first, then Eve.' The appeal to creation order grounds the directive of v.12 in Gen 2:7, 21-23. Eplasthē (was formed) — the aorist passive of plassō (to form, mold, as a potter), the LXX word for YHWH's forming Adam from the dust (Gen 2:7-8). The primogeniture argument (firstborn = greater authority) is a Jewish hermeneutical principle used also by Paul in 1 Cor 11:8-9 and 15:45-47. Whether the appeal is to creation order as permanent ontological hierarchy or to the particular situation of the fall's effects is the hermeneutical crux of the passage.
2.14: kai Adam ouk ēpatēthē, hē de gunē exapatēthheisa en parabasei gegonen — 'and Adam was not deceived, but the woman was deceived and became a transgressor.' Exapatēthheisa (thoroughly deceived) — the compound verb exapataō intensifies simple deception (cf. 2 Cor 11:3 where Paul uses the same word of the serpent's deception of Eve). The argument is not that women are inherently more susceptible to deception — Adam's sin (cf. Rom 5:12-21 where Adam bears the primary responsibility) was deliberate disobedience rather than deception. The passage uses the deception-first narrative (Gen 3:1-6) to ground the teaching-authority principle in the fall's dynamics, not in ontological inferiority.
2.15: sōthēsetai de dia tēs teknogonias — 'but she will be saved through childbearing.' The most puzzling verse in 1 Timothy. Sōthēsetai could mean: (a) eschatologically saved — if so, by works of childbearing, which contradicts Pauline soteriology; (b) preserved/kept safe through the process of childbearing — a physical promise; (c) saved through the childbearing — i.e., through Christ's birth (the definite article tēs teknogonias suggests 'the childbearing'), making this a Christological reference to Gen 3:15 (the seed of the woman). Option (c) has minority support but makes best theological sense: women are saved by the same gospel as men — through the birth of the Messiah from woman. The switch to plural 'if they remain' signals the ongoing condition for all women, not just Eve.

3.16: homologoumenos mega estin to tes eusebeias mysterion: 'Great indeed, as we confess, is the mystery of godliness.' What follows (vv. 16b-c) is almost certainly a pre-Pauline hymn or creedal fragment in six lines, possibly arranged in two strophes of three: (1) manifested in flesh / vindicated by Spirit / seen by angels; (2) proclaimed among nations / believed in the world / taken up in glory. The six verbs are all aorist passives — divine actions done to Christ. The structure mirrors Phil 2:6-11's humiliation-exaltation pattern: the first triad (incarnation, vindication, heavenly witness) parallels the descending and ascending of the Christ-hymn.
3.1: pistos ho logos: ei tis episkopēs oregetai kalou ergou epithumei — 'trustworthy is the saying: if anyone aspires to oversight, he desires a good work.' The second pistos ho logos formula introduces the overseer-standard as a recognized community principle. Episkopē (oversight, the office of overseer) — the noun from which episkopos comes; the office, not merely the function. Oregetai (aspire, reach after) and epithumei (desire earnestly) are both present tenses: the desire for the office is ongoing and appropriate. The key phrase: it is a kalon ergon (good work/noble task) — the honor of the office is inseparable from its labor.
3.2: dei oun ton episkopon anepilēmpton einai — 'the overseer must therefore be beyond reproach.' Anepilēmptos (beyond reproach, without basis for accusation) — not sinless but without a sustained vulnerable point that opponents can attack. The qualification list that follows is not primarily doctrinal but character-based: the overseer's fitness is demonstrated by their life before appointment. Mias gynaikos andra (husband of one wife, lit. 'a one-woman man') — the phrase is much debated: prohibition of polygamy? Of remarriage after divorce? Of remarriage after a spouse's death? Most likely a reputation for marital fidelity and moral consistency rather than a specific marital history requirement.
3.3: mē paroinon mē plēktēn alla epieikē amachon aphilarguron — 'not a drunkard, not violent, but gentle, peaceable, not a lover of money.' The series of negatives and positives are character-virtues recognizable in both Jewish and Hellenistic leadership ethics: epieikēs (gentle, reasonable, equitable — a Hellenistic virtue term, Phil 4:5) and amachos (not contentious, peaceable) contrast with the violence and aggression that disqualified. Aphilarguros (not a lover of money, free from avarice) — the philarguros (lover of money) is identified in 6:10 as the root of all evils: the overseer must be structurally free from the vice that corrupts leadership.
3.4: tou idiou oikou kalōs proistamenon — 'managing his own household well.' Proistēmi (to stand before, to lead, to manage) is the same word used for community leaders in 1 Thess 5:12. The analogy is household → church: competence in leading the smaller unit qualifies (and failing to lead the smaller disqualifies) for leading the larger. Tekna echonta en hypotagē meta pasēs semnotētos (having children in submission with all dignity) — the children's submission is evidence of the father's leadership competence, not merely their own virtue. The father takes responsibility for the community character of his household.
3.5: ei de tis tou idiou oikou proistasthai ouk oiden, pōs ekklēsias theou epimelēsetai — 'if someone does not know how to manage his own household, how will he care for God's church?' The a minori ad maius argument (from lesser to greater): the church is harder to lead than a household, so household leadership competence is the minimum qualification. Epimelēsetai (will care for/attend to) shifts the metaphor from management to pastoral care — attending to needs, nurturing. The church is 'God's household' (oikos theou, 3:15), so the qualification for God's house must exceed what qualifies for a human one.
3.6: mē neophyton, hina mē tuphotheIs krima empesē tou diabolou — 'not a recent convert, lest being puffed up he fall into the condemnation of the devil.' Neophutos (newly planted, new convert) — a horticultural metaphor for someone recently planted in the faith, lacking deep root. Tuphōtheis (being puffed up, inflated with conceit) — the participle of tuphoō, used of smoke or vapor that inflates without substance. The genitive 'of the devil' (tou diabolou) in 'condemnation of the devil' may mean: the same judgment that the devil received (the devil's own condemnation) or the judgment the devil brings to puffed-up leaders.
3.7: dei de auton kai martyrian kalēn echein apo tōn exōthen — 'he must also have a good testimony from outsiders.' Exōthen (from outside — those not in the community) — the overseer's reputation must survive scrutiny from people who have no interest in favorably evaluating him. The twofold danger in v.7 — 'fall into disgrace' (oneidismon, reproach) and 'the devil's trap' (pagida tou diabolou) — uses the same pagida (snare/trap) that 6:9 applies to people who desire to be rich: the devil sets structural traps through social vulnerabilities.
3.8: diakonous hōsautōs semnous — 'deacons likewise must be dignified.' Semnous (dignified, serious, honorable) — the quality of gravitas appropriate to someone in public service. The deacon-list parallels the overseer-list with some differences: no requirement for ability to teach (since deacons were not primarily teaching roles), but addition of 'dilogous' (double-tongued/saying different things to different people) as a specific vice to avoid — relevant to deacons who served as messengers and household-visitors in the early church. Oinō pollō prosechontas (not given to much wine) — the comparative suggests the concern is excess, not total abstinence.
3.9: echontas to mystērion tēs pisteōs en kathara suneidēsei — 'holding the mystery of the faith with a clear conscience.' To mystērion tēs pisteōs (the mystery of the faith) — the revealed content of Christian belief, paralleling the 'mystery of godliness' in v.16. The deacon must hold this doctrinal deposit, but the manner of holding it — en kathara suneidēsei (in a clean conscience) — ensures that knowledge and moral character remain integrated. A deacon who has the doctrinal knowledge but a defiled conscience is disqualified by the same logic as the false teachers of ch.1 who have deviated from a clean heart and good conscience.
3.10: kai houtoi de dokimazesthōsan prōton, eita diakoneitōsan anenklētoi ontes — 'let them also be tested first; then let them serve as deacons if they are blameless.' Dokimazesthōsan (let them be tested, proven) — the present passive imperative signals an ongoing evaluation process before formal appointment. Prōton (first) distinguishes deacon appointment from overseer appointment: there is a testing phase between candidacy and office. Anenklētoi (without accusation, blameless — used in Titus 1:6 for elders) — the same accountability standard as the overseer applies.
3.11: gynaikas hōsautōs semnas mē diabolous nēphalious pistas en pasin — 'women likewise must be dignified, not slanderers, sober-minded, faithful in all things.' The referent is disputed: wives of deacons or a separate order of women deacons (female deacons)? The parallel structure (hōsautōs — likewise — introduces the women after both the overseer and deacon paragraphs) and the character-list format (paralleling the deacon-list) suggests these are women serving a diaconal function. The specific vice 'diabolous' (slanderers — lit. 'devils,' the same word as 'the devil') was a known risk for those doing pastoral house-to-house visitation: gossip and slander destroy the trust such ministry requires.
3.12: diakonoi estōsan mias gynaikos andres tōn idiōn teknōn kalōs proistamenoi kai tōn idiōn oikōn — 'let deacons be husbands of one wife, managing their children and their own households well.' The same mias gynaikos andra (one-woman man) requirement as for overseers; the same household-management criterion. The parallelism between overseer and deacon qualifications at this point suggests both are full-time ministry roles with structural accountability, not merely volunteer positions.
3.13: hoi gar kalōs diakonēsantes bathmon heautois kalon peripoiountai kai pollēn parrēsian en pistei tē en Christō Iēsou — 'for those who serve well as deacons gain a good standing for themselves and great boldness in the faith that is in Christ Jesus.' Bathmon kalon (good standing — lit. 'a good step/grade') — the metaphor of a step suggests advancement, recognition, perhaps eventual movement to overseer-level leadership. Parrēsia (boldness, frank speech, confidence) — the freedom of speech that belongs to those with nothing to hide: the deacon who has served faithfully can speak with authority in the community.
3.14: tauta soi graphō elpizōn elthein pros se en tachei — 'I write these things to you, hoping to come to you soon.' The letter situates itself as a substitute for Paul's delayed presence: the written instructions must serve in place of the apostle's personal direction. En tachei (soon, quickly) — Paul's hope of rapid return; the conditional 'if I am delayed' (v.15) acknowledges uncertainty. The letter's governing purpose is stated in v.15: so that Timothy will know how people ought to behave in God's household — the instructions are not personal advice but community-ordering guidance.
3.15: stulos kai hedraiōma tēs alētheias — 'pillar and foundation of the truth.' These two images apply to the church as the community that upholds and undergirds truth. Stulos (pillar — a weight-bearing column, as in the pillars of a temple) supports the truth from below; hedraiōma (buttress, foundation, firm base) stabilizes it. The metaphors answer the false teachers: when the community's teaching authority is properly ordered, the truth stands; when it is disordered, the truth is threatened. The church does not generate truth but holds and displays it.

6.15: ho makarios kai monos dynamastes ho basileus ton basileonton kai kyrios ton kyrieonton (ho makarios kai monos dynastēs, ho basileus tōn basileuontōn kai kyrios tōn kyrieuontōn): 'the blessed and only Sovereign, the King of kings and Lord of lords.' The doxology of 6:15-16 applies to the epiphaneia of Jesus the titles that Hellenistic rulers and the Roman emperor claimed. Dynastēs (Sovereign/potentate) was used of divine-right rulers in the Hellenistic east. King of kings and Lord of lords echoes Deut 10:17 (YHWH as God of gods and Lord of lords) and Daniel 2:37, 47 (the God of heaven as King over kings). The doxology places the Roman emperor's pretensions under the sovereignty of Christ.
6.1: Hosoi eisin hypo zygon douloi (all who are under the yoke of slavery): hypo zygon (under the yoke) — an agricultural metaphor for the condition of the enslaved; the yoke evokes the heavy labor of draft animals. The instruction is addressed to slaves with non-believing masters (v.1) and then with believing masters (v.2), showing the community included both configurations. The rationale for respectful conduct is explicitly missional: so that to onoma tou theou kai hē didaskalia mē blasphēmētai (God's name and the teaching may not be slandered). The gospel's credibility in the wider Greco-Roman society is the reason for the social conduct requirement.
6.2: Mē kataphroneitsōsan hoti adelphoi eisin, alla mallon douleuetsōsan hoti pistoi eisin kai agapētoi hoi tēs euergesias antilambanos (let them not despise them because they are brothers, but serve them more fully because those who receive the benefit are faithful and beloved): the instruction counterintuitively does not say 'brothers are equals, so the slave relationship dissolves' but 'brothers are even more worthy of full service.' Euergesia (benefit, good service) and antilambanos (receiving, taking hold of) — the benefactor vocabulary suggests that the believing master's gracious reception of service should motivate increased diligence rather than exploitation of the relationship.
6.3: Ei tis heterodidaskalei kai mē proserchetai hygiainousi logois tois tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou (if anyone teaches otherwise and does not agree with the sound words of our Lord Jesus Christ): heterodidaskalei (teaches otherwise/different things) — the same verb as 1:3; the false teacher is defined negatively by deviation from apostolic teaching. Hygianousin logois (sound/healthy words) — hygianō (to be healthy) applied to teaching is characteristic of the Pastoral Epistles (1 Tim 1:10; 6:3; Tit 1:9; 2:1; 2 Tim 1:13; 4:3); teaching is either healthy or diseased. Kata eusebeian (according to godliness) — the standard against which teaching is measured is practical godliness, not abstract correctness.
6.4: Tetyphōtai, mēden epistamenos, alla nosōn peri zētēseis kai logomachias (he is conceited, knowing nothing, but being diseased about controversies and word-battles): tetyphōtai (is conceited/has been made smoke-blind) — the perfect passive of typhōō (to cloud with smoke/vapor); the false teacher is intellectually blinded by self-inflation. Nosōn (being diseased/sick) — noseō extended from physical illness to intellectual-moral disorder; the false teacher is pathologically attracted to controversies. Logomachias (word-battles) — a hapax legomenon in the NT; logomachia = battles over words with no substantive content.
6.5: Porizmon einai tēn eusebeian (godliness as a means of profit/livelihood): porizmos (profit, financial gain, means of livelihood) — a commercial term; the false teachers have turned religious activity into a revenue stream. The charge of mercenary religion is a standard polemical accusation in the Greco-Roman world (Cynics, sophists, and Jewish teachers were all accused of teaching for money). Paul's charge is not that the teachers receive support (cf. 5:17-18: elders worthy of double honor) but that they have made financial gain the motivating goal of their teaching.
6.6: Estin de porizmos megas hē eusebeia meta autarkeias (but godliness with contentment/self-sufficiency is great gain): the letter reverses the false teachers' equation. Autarkeia (contentment, self-sufficiency) — a core Stoic philosophical virtue: the wise person who is autarkēs is independent of external circumstances, sufficient within themselves through cultivated virtue. Paul adopts the Stoic vocabulary but regrounds it: godliness (relation to God) rather than inner virtue is the source of true sufficiency. The Stoic goal of autarkeia is fulfilled not by philosophical training but by godliness (cf. Phil 4:11: Paul has 'learned' autarkein through union with Christ).
6.7: Ouden gar eisēnegkamen eis ton kosmon, hoti ouden dynametha kai exenegkein (for we brought nothing into the world, and we are unable to take anything out of it): the universally recognized truth about human mortality and possessionlessness. The closest parallels are Job 1:21 ('naked I came from my mother's womb, and naked I will depart') and Eccles 5:15 ('as they came from their mother's womb, so they will depart'). The maxim grounds the sufficiency-with-godliness teaching in the ontological reality of human existence: accumulation has no ultimate future.
6.8: Echontes de diatrophas kai skepas, toutois arskesthēsometha (but having food and clothing, we will be content with these): diatrophas (food, sustenance) and skepas (covering, shelter/clothing) — the two basic physical necessities. The pairing echoes Stoic and Cynic diatribes on the minimal requirements for a life of virtue, and also the wisdom tradition (Prov 30:8: 'Give me neither poverty nor riches'). The conditional minimal sufficiency — if we have these, we will be content — is the practical application of autarkeia grounded in godliness.
6.9:
Hoi de boulomenoi ploutein empipto
usin eis peirasmon kai pagida (those who want to get rich fall into temptation and a trap): the participial phrase boulomenoi ploutein (wanting to get rich) identifies the posture of wealth-craving, not wealth itself, as the danger. Peirasmon kai pagida (temptation and trap) — two images: the internal testing that pulls toward what destroys, and the external snare of the trapper. Epithumias pollas anōētous kai blaberas, hai bythi
zousi tous anthrōpous eis olethron kai apōleian (many foolish and harmful desires that plunge people into ruin and destruction) — bythizousi (to plunge/sink, literally submerge) is a nautical term for sinking a ship.
6.10: Rhiza gar pantōn tōn kakōn estin hē philarguria (for a root of all the evils is the love of money): philarguria (love of money/silver; philos + argyros) — the Pastoral Epistles' formulation is often misquoted as 'money is the root of all evil'; the actual claim is that love of money is a root of all kinds of evil (not the root of all evil without qualification). The Greco-Roman moralist tradition had similar formulations. Hēs tines oregomenoi (craving which some have) — oregomai (to reach out for, crave) is a different verb from epithumia (desire), suggesting the active reaching-out for what one does not yet possess. Apeplanethesan apo tēs pisteōs kai heautous periepeiran odynais pollais (have wandered from the faith and pierced themselves with many pains) — periepeiran (pierced through, impaled) is a vivid self-inflicted wound image.
6.11: Sy de, ō anthrōpe theou, tauta pheuge (but you, O man of God, flee these things): anthrōpe theou (man of God) — an OT prophetic title applied to Moses (Deut 33:1), Samuel (1 Sam 9:6-8), Elijah (1 Kgs 17:18, 24), Elisha (2 Kgs 4:9), and other prophets; its application to Timothy elevates him to the prophetic tradition of covenant loyalty and counter-cultural faithfulness. Diōke de dikaiosynēn, eusebeian, pistin, agapēn, hypomonēn, prautēta (pursue righteousness, godliness, faith, love, endurance, gentleness) — the six-term virtue list balances the flight from vice (v.11a) with the pursuit of virtue; diōkō (pursue) is the same verb used for persecution, implying active energetic pursuit.
6.12: Agōnizou ton kalon agōna tēs pisteōs (fight/contend the good contest/fight of faith): the athletic and military contest vocabulary (agōnizomai, agōn) is concentrated in the Pastoral Epistles and elsewhere in Paul (Col 1:29; 4:12; Phil 1:30; 2:16; 1 Cor 9:25-27). Epilaboū tēs aiōniou zōēs, eis hēn eklēthēs kai hōmologēsas tēn kalēn homologian enōpion pollōn martyrōn (take hold of eternal life, to which you were called and confessed the good confession before many witnesses): homologian (confession, declaration of agreement) — probably a baptismal or ordination confession; enōpion pollōn martyrōn (before many witnesses) suggests a public liturgical context.
6.13: Parangellō soi enōpion tou theou tou zōogonountos ta panta kai Christou Iēsou tou martyrēsantos epi Pontiou Pilatou tēn kalēn homologian (I charge you before God who gives life to all things and Christ Jesus who testified the good confession before Pontius Pilate): zōogonountos ta panta (who gives life to all things) — the Creator-God as the ground of the solemn charge; the one who gives all life is the witness. Pontius Pilate (epi Pontiou Pilatou) — one of the rare explicit historical datings in the Pastoral Epistles (26-36 CE); Christ's testimony before Pilate (martyrēsas tēn kalēn homologian) is the paradigm for Timothy's public confession. The parallel between Christ's testimony before Pilate and Timothy's confession before witnesses (v.12) is structurally deliberate.
6.14: Tērēsai se tēn entolēn aspilon anepilēmpton mechri tēs epiphaneias tou Kyriou hēmōn Iēsou Christou (to keep the command without spot and unimpeachable until the appearing of our Lord Jesus Christ): tēn entolēn (the commandment) — the singular may refer to the whole body of apostolic instruction or specifically the charge given to Timothy. Aspilon anepilēmpton (without spot, unimpeachable) — the dual compound is near-liturgical; the language echoes sacrificial purity requirements (amōmos = without blemish, Heb 9:14) and judicial acquittal language. Epiphaneia (appearing) — the Pastoral Epistles' distinctive Christological term (2 Tim 1:10; 4:1, 8; Tit 2:13; cf. 2 Thess 2:8), used for both the first coming (2 Tim 1:10) and the Parousia. It is also the Hellenistic technical term for the manifestation of a deity or the public appearance of a king.
6.16: Ho monos echōn athanasian, phōs oikōn aprositon, hon eiden oudeis anthrōpōn oude idein dynatai (who alone has immortality, dwelling in unapproachable light, whom no human has seen or is able to see): monos echōn athanasian (who alone has immortality) — God's immortality (athanasia) is underived and intrinsic, not shared by creation; the angels and humans who participate in immortality do so derivatively. Phōs oikōn aprositon (dwelling in unapproachable light) — combines the OT theophanies of the divine glory as fire and light (Exod 24:17: consuming fire; Ps 104:2: wrapped in light as a garment; 1 Kgs 8:12: YHWH dwelling in thick darkness) with the Hellenistic philosophical tradition of the divine as pure light. Hon eiden oudeis anthrōpōn (whom no human has seen) — Exod 33:20 (no one may see me and live); John 1:18 (no one has seen God at any time).
6.17: Tois plousiois en tō nyn aiōni (those who are rich in the present age): en tō nyn aiōni (in the present age) — the eschatological qualifier frames earthly wealth as age-specific: it belongs to the current order of things, which is passing. Hypeorphroneō (to be arrogant/think too highly of oneself) and ēlpikenas epi ploutou adēlotēti (having set hope on the uncertainty of wealth) — two linked dangers: the pride that wealth generates and the misplaced security it appears to offer. Adēlotēs ploutou (uncertainty/instability of wealth) — wealth as a fundamentally unreliable foundation for hope.
6.18: Agathoergein, ploutein en ergois kalois, eumetadotous einai, koinōnikous (to do good, to be rich in good works, to be generous, to be willing to share): the four-fold instruction redirects the wealthy from passive possession to active generosity. Koinōnikous (willing to share, generous in sharing) — from koinōnia (fellowship, sharing): the wealthy are to practice the community-partnership that is the letter's social ideal. Eumetadotous (generous, liberal in giving) — eu (well) + metadidōmi (share out); the compounding emphasizes ready and generous sharing, not grudging donation.
6.19:
Apothēsaurizonta
s heautois themelion kalon eis to mellon, hina epilabōntai tēs ontōs zōēs (storing up for themselves a good foundation for the future, so that they may take hold of the life that is really life): apothēsaurizō (to store up as treasure) — the same root as thēsauros (treasure); the wealthy who invest in good works are making an eschatological deposit. Tēs ontōs zōēs (the life that really is life) — the genitive of emphasis: authentic life, contrasted with the mere biological existence of the present age. The 'taking hold' (epilabōntai) mirrors the same verb applied to Timothy in v.12 (take hold of eternal life): the same eschatological grasp is available to those who invest in the kingdom rather than hoarding temporal wealth.
6.20: Ō Timothee, tēn parathēkēn phylaxon (O Timothy, guard the deposit): parathēkē (deposit entrusted for safekeeping) — a banking and legal term: the deposit made with a trusted party who is responsible for returning it intact. The apostolic teaching-tradition is a deposit entrusted to Timothy; he is the custodian, not the owner. Tas bebēlous kenophōnias kai antitheseis tēs pseudōnymou gnōseōs (godless empty chatter and the opposing arguments of what is falsely named knowledge): pseudōnymos (falsely named) applied to gnōsis (knowledge) — the false teachers' system is called 'knowledge' but is falsely labeled; it lacks true knowledge. Whether this is proto-Gnosticism or a local form of Jewish esoteric speculation, the epistemological claim is refuted by the label itself.
6.21: Hēn tines epangellomenoi peri tēn pistin ēstochēsan (which some professing have missed the mark regarding the faith): ēstochēsan (missed the mark, erred) — from astocheō (to miss the target); same verb as 1:6 (having turned aside) and 2 Tim 2:18 (who have swerved from the truth). The closing hē charis meth' hymōn (grace be with you) — the standard Pauline letter-closing formula, here in the plural 'you' (hymōn), indicating the letter's address extends beyond Timothy to the broader congregation at Ephesus.

1.1: apostolos Christou Iēsou kat' epitagēn theou — 'apostle of Christ Jesus by command (epitagē) of God.' Epitagē (command, authoritative order) is stronger than thelēma (will) used in Paul's earlier letters (1 Cor 1:1; 2 Cor 1:1; Eph 1:1). The shift to 'command' language signals the Pastoral Epistles' heightened concern with apostolic authority and the legitimacy of the tradition Paul is transmitting. Sōtēros hēmōn theou (God our Savior) — the title 'Savior' applied to God is characteristically Pastoral (1 Tim 2:3; 4:10; Titus 1:3; 2:10; 3:4) and reflects the LXX's regular use of sōtēr for YHWH as deliverer. Christ Jesus our hope: elpis is personalized — not a thing hoped for but a person.
1.2: gnēsiō teknō en pistei — 'true son (gnēsios — legitimate, genuine, not illegitimate) in the faith.' The word distinguishes Timothy from false teachers who claim apostolic authority without genuine relationship or commission. The triple benediction 'grace, mercy, peace' (charis, eleos, eirēnē) expands the standard Pauline opening 'grace and peace' with eleos (mercy) — the distinctively OT covenantal term for YHWH's steadfast loyalty (hesed). Its addition in the Pastoral salutations (also 2 Tim 1:2; Titus 1:4 lacks it) may signal the heightened vulnerability of Timothy's situation: he needs mercy as well as grace.
1.3: heterodidaskalein — 'teach differently' (heteros = different in kind, not allos = different in number). The compound is a Pauline coinage (appearing again in 6:3) marking the false teachers' problem as categorical difference from the apostolic norm, not merely minor variation. The false teaching is not identified by name but characterized functionally: it promotes muthois (myths) and genealogiais (genealogies). 'Myths and genealogies' likely refers to Jewish speculative interpretation involving OT genealogies developed in Second Temple literature — elaborations beyond what the text says that generate controversy rather than edification (oikodomia).
1.4: muthois kai genealogiais aperantois — 'myths and endless genealogies.' Aperantois (endless, boundless) describes content that generates only ekzētēseis (controversies, investigations) rather than oikonomian theou (God's stewardship — some MSS read oikodomēn, edification). The contrast is between speculation that multiplies questions and the gospel that builds up. The participial parechousin (promote, furnish) makes the myths and genealogies themselves the agents of the harm — the content is structurally corrosive, not merely peripheral.
1.5: to de telos tēs paraggeleias estin agapē — 'the goal (telos) of the command is love.' Telos is end/goal in the teleological sense — love is not incidental to the instruction but its intended terminus. The love described is then triple-sourced: from a katharas kardias (clean heart — the OT moral center, Ps 51:10), an agathēs suneidēseōs (good conscience — the Hellenistic-Jewish moral self-awareness Paul develops throughout the Pastorals), and an anupokritou pisteōs (sincere/unhypocritical faith). The three-source structure answers the false teachers' problem: they have neither clean hearts nor good consciences nor unhypocritical faith, which is why their teaching produces controversy rather than love.
1.6: hōn tines astochēsantes — 'which some having missed/deviated from.' Astocheō (to miss the mark, deviate from the target) is a Pastoral neologism (also 1 Tim 6:21; 2 Tim 2:18). The image is of an archer or a person on a path who fails to hit or follow the right course. The deviation is from the triple-source of love (v. 5), not from doctrine directly — the false teachers' error is moral before it is doctrinal. They 'turned' (exetrapēsan — aorist passive of ektrepō, to turn aside) to mataiologia (meaningless talk, empty discourse) — a term from the LXX applied to idol-worship.
1.7: thelontes einai nomodidaskaloi mē noountes — 'wanting to be teachers of the law, not understanding.' The aorist participle noountes (understanding/comprehending) is negated: they lack the understanding that would qualify them for what they desire. Nomodidaskalos (law-teacher) appears in Luke 5:17 and Acts 5:34 (Gamaliel) for recognized Jewish scribal teachers. The false teachers want this honorable title without the knowledge it requires. The emphatic mē noountes mēte ha legousin mēte peri tinōn diabebaiountai — 'not understanding either what they are saying or what things they confidently assert' — piles up the negations: double ignorance about both content and reference.
1.8: oida de hoti kalos ho nomos ean tis autō nomimōs chrētai — 'we know that the law is good if one uses it lawfully/legitimately.' The adverb nomimōs (according to law, lawfully) is a rhetorical paradox: the law must be used law-fully. The principle sets up the next verses: the law functions differently for different categories of people. For the righteous, it is not given (ou keitai — not laid down); for the unrighteous, it is the diagnostic instrument that reveals and names their sin.
1.9: oti dikaiō nomos ou keitai — 'that law is not laid down for a just person.' The dikaios (righteous/just) person does not need the law as an external coercive boundary — they live its requirement by nature of their character transformation. The catalogue of sinners that follows (vv. 9-10) is structured in roughly legal/Decalogal order: the first three pairs (lawless and rebellious / ungodly and sinful / unholy and profane) echo the first table of the Decalog (duties toward God); the subsequent pairs (patricides/matricides, murderers, sexually immoral, homosexuals, slave-traders, liars) echo the second table (duties toward neighbor). The catalogue diagnoses society as needing law precisely because it is lawless.
1.10: arsenokoitais — 'those who practice homosexuality' (ESV); 'men who have sex with men' (NIV); 'sodomites' (NRSV). The term is a compound of arsēn (male) + koitē (bed/lying) and appears first in the NT (here and 1 Cor 6:9) and in scattered later Greek sources. It may be a coinage based on the LXX of Lev 18:22 and 20:13 (arsenos koitēn). The semantic range is debated: some limit it to male prostitution or pederasty; others read it as a broad term for same-sex sexual activity between males. The parallel pornois (sexually immoral) suggests a broader sexual ethics category. The phrase 'and whatever else is contrary to sound doctrine' (hygiainousē didaskalia) — healthy/health-producing teaching — frames all the catalogue entries as violations of the doctrinal health-standard.
1.11: to euangelion tēs doxēs tou makariou theou — 'the gospel of the glory of the blessed God.' The genitive chain: gospel ← of the glory ← of the blessed God. The gospel is characterized by glory (doxa) — not merely information but a disclosure of divine splendor. Makarios (blessed/happy) applied to God is unusual in the NT (only here and 6:15); it derives from the LXX and Hellenistic usage. Paul's addition 'which was entrusted to me' — the perfect passive episte(u)thēn — marks the gospel as a trust held in fiduciary relationship: Paul does not own it but stewards it.
1.12: charin echō tō endunamōsanti me Christō Iēsou tō kyriō hēmōn — 'I give thanks to Christ Jesus our Lord who strengthened me.' The aorist participle endunamōsanti (having empowered/strengthened) points to Christ's past act of equipping Paul for ministry. The triple designation — 'Christ Jesus our Lord' — is solemn. Pistos me hēgēsato (he considered me faithful/trustworthy) — the aorist indicates a past divine assessment. God's assessment of trustworthiness precedes and creates the appointment: Paul did not become trustworthy before being appointed; the appointment itself was the act of divine grace that created the trustworthiness.
1.13: to proteron onta blasphēmon kai diōktēn kai hubristēn — 'formerly being a blasphemer and a persecutor and an insolent man.' The three terms escalate: blasphēmos (blasphemer) — speaking against God and Christ; diōktēs (persecutor) — hunting down the church; hubristēs (violent aggressor, insolent one) — the term covers both arrogance and its violent expression. The softening — ēleēthēn hoti agnoōn epoiēsa en apistiā (I was shown mercy because I acted ignorantly in unbelief) — distinguishes Paul's sin from the willful apostasy of 6:20-21: ignorance-based sin is in a different category than deliberate rejection of known truth (cf. Num 15:30 on the distinction between inadvertent and high-handed sin).
1.14: hyperepleonasen de hē charis tou kyriou hēmōn — 'the grace of our Lord overflowed beyond measure.' The compound hyperepleonasen (super-abounded/overflowed) is a Pauline coinage (hapax in NT) that intensifies the abundance already expressed in perisseuō. Grace did not merely appear but exceeded all expectation of measure — it overflowed the container of Paul's sin. The accompanying gifts — faith and love en Christō Iēsou — are not Paul's own virtues but qualities that exist in Christ and flow to him in union with Christ: the faith and love of the Christian are participatory, not self-generated.
1.15: pistos ho logos kai pasēs apodochēs axios — 'trustworthy is the saying and worthy of full acceptance.' The formula pistos ho logos introduces five Pastoral Epistle sayings (1 Tim 1:15; 3:1; 4:9; 2 Tim 2:11; Titus 3:8), marking each as a recognized and authoritative statement within the community — perhaps early creedal or catechetical formulations. The saying here: Christos Iēsous ēlthen eis ton kosmon hamartōlous sōsai — Christ Jesus came into the world to save sinners. Ēlthen eis ton kosmon (came into the world) implies pre-existence and mission: Christ entered a world he was not originating from. The present-tense self-identification hōn prōtos eimi egō (of whom I am the foremost/first) — Paul does not say 'I was the foremost' but present tense: his continued awareness of the depth of his sin is the ground of ongoing marvel at grace.
1.16: alla dia touto ēleēthēn hina en emoi prōtō endeixētai Christos Iēsous tēn hapasan makrothumian — 'for this reason I received mercy, so that in me, as the foremost, Christ Jesus might display his complete patience.' Endeixētai (might display/demonstrate) — the aorist subjunctive of purpose: Paul's conversion was not merely personal salvation but a public demonstration for future generations. Makrothumia (patience, long-suffering, slow to anger) is the word that in the LXX describes YHWH's restraint of judgment (Exod 34:6; Num 14:18). Christ's 'complete' (hapasan) patience displayed in Paul becomes the exemplar (hypotypōsin — pattern, prototype) for all future believers.
1.17: tō de basilei tōn aiōnōn aphtartō aoratō monō theō timē kai doxa eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — 'to the King of the ages, immortal, invisible, the only God.' The doxology breaks from the personal testimony with a liturgical outburst. Basileus tōn aiōnōn (King of the ages/eternity) — the genitive may be temporal (reigning over the ages) or qualitative (the eternal King). Aphtartos (incorruptible, immortal) and aoratos (invisible) apply to God the qualities that Paul elsewhere ascribes to resurrection bodies and to God's eternal nature (Rom 1:20). The doxology's form — timē kai doxa eis tous aiōnas tōn aiōnōn — is the standard Jewish synagogue closing blessing adapted to Christological worship.
1.18: tautēn tēn paraggellian paratithemai soi teknon Timothee — 'this command I entrust to you, child Timothy.' Paratithemai (to place beside, entrust) is the fiduciary verb of depositing a trust: the command is a parathēkē (deposit) that Timothy must guard (cf. 6:20's tēn parathēkēn phulaxon). The phrase kata tas proagousas epi se prophēteias (in accordance with the prophecies previously made about you) refers to prophetic words that accompanied Timothy's commissioning — possibly at his ordination (4:14). Paul grounds the command in divine authorization: the commission Timothy received is not Paul's opinion but follows a prophetic pattern already spoken over him.
1.19: echōn pistin kai agathēn suneidēsin hēn tines apōsamenoi — 'holding faith and a good conscience, which some have rejected.' Apōsamenoi (having thrust away, rejected) — the aorist middle of apōtheō, used of deliberate, forceful rejection. The rejection was not passive drift but active refusal of conscience. The result: peri tēn pistin enauagēsan (they have shipwrecked with regard to the faith). The shipwreck metaphor is vivid — not a gentle drifting off course but a catastrophic structural failure. Conscience and faith are linked: when conscience is suppressed, faith does not survive independently.
1.20: hous paredōka tō Satana hina paideuthosin mē blasphēmein — 'whom I have handed over to Satan so that they may be taught not to blaspheme.' The disciplinary formula echoes 1 Cor 5:5 (the immoral man handed to Satan for destruction of the flesh). The paradox: Satan, the adversary, becomes the instrument of corrective discipline. The goal is remedial (paideuthosin — the verb of parental discipline and education, used in Heb 12:6-11 for divine chastening) not punitive: the outcome sought is that they 'learn not to blaspheme.' The naming of Hymenaeus and Alexander (real, traceable individuals) marks this as public ecclesial discipline, not private pastoral concern.

4.1: to de pneuma rētōs legei hoti en hysterois kairois apostēsontai tines tēs pisteōs — 'the Spirit explicitly says that in later times some will depart from the faith.' Rētōs (explicitly, in so many words) — the Spirit's utterance is clear and specific, not vague. Whether this refers to a specific prophecy received in the community or to prophetic texts from the OT/Jesus tradition is unclear. Hysterois kairois (later times) — the same eschatological category as eschatais hēmerais (last days) in 2 Tim 3:1. Paul applies this future warning to the present Ephesian situation: the 'later times' are now. Apostēsontai tēs pisteōs (will depart from the faith) — the apostasia of 2 Thess 2:3 at the individual level.
4.2: en hypokrisei pseudologōn kekautēriasmenōn tēn idian suneidēsin — 'through the hypocrisy of liars whose own consciences are seared.' Kekautēriasmenōn (seared, branded with a hot iron) — the perfect passive participle indicates a completed action with ongoing results: their consciences were seared and remain deadened. The branding metaphor suggests permanent damage to the moral sensory apparatus. The hypokrisei (hypocrisy) connects to the false teachers' public presentation vs. their internal state: they perform spiritual authority while their consciences are cauterized.
4.3: kōluontōn gamein, apechesthai brōmatōn — 'forbidding marriage, commanding abstinence from foods.' The two ascetic prohibitions are the concrete content of the false teaching. In Jewish thought, both marriage and food were created goods; the false teachers' prohibition of them implies a negative view of the material order. Paul's counter: ha ho theos ektisen eis metalēmpsin meta eucharistias (which God created to be received with thanksgiving) — creation is the ground of the refutation. What God created as good cannot be intrinsically defiling. The eschatological language: the error of these teachers treats the material world as the enemy of spiritual progress.
4.4: hoti pan ktisma theou kalon kai ouden apoblēton — 'for everything God created is good and nothing is to be rejected.' The Pauline creatio ex nihilo affirmation has ethical consequences: the goodness of creation establishes the goodness of what is created. Apoblēton (to be rejected, thrown away) — the food-purity laws of Judaism and the asceticism of the false teachers are both put under the theological criterion of creation: if God made it, rejecting it without warrant contradicts the Creator's assessment.
4.5: hagiazetai gar dia logou theou kai enteuxeōs — 'for it is sanctified by the word of God and prayer.' Hagiazetai (is sanctified, made holy) — the present passive indicates an ongoing process, not a one-time event. The mechanism of sanctification: logos theou (word of God — the creation narrative of Gen 1 which calls everything good, and possibly the blessing prayer over food) + enteuxis (intercession/prayer). The blessing of food before eating is thus theologically significant: it enacts the acknowledgment that what God created is good, reversing the false teachers' implicit rejection of creation.
4.6: tauta hypotitheios tois adelphois kalos esē diakonos Christou Iēsou — 'by pointing these things out to the brothers you will be a good servant of Christ Jesus.' Hypotitheios (putting under, suggesting, laying before) — a gentle rather than authoritative verb: Timothy is not commanded to enforce but to set before the community. Enentrapheis tois logois tēs pisteōs kai tēs kalēs didaskalias hē parēkolouthēkas (nourished on the words of the faith and the good teaching you have followed) — the metaphor: spiritual nourishment from doctrinal content is what equips for ministry, paralleling physical nourishment.
4.7: tous de bebēlous kai graōdeis muthous paraitou — 'have nothing to do with irreverent, silly myths.' Bebēlous (profane, worldly — the opposite of hagios/holy) characterizes the myths as without sacred substance. Graōdeis (old-womanish — a Hellenistic put-down for superstitious, credulous, uncritical beliefs; cf. Plutarch's use of the term for folk superstitions) is blunt condescension toward the false teaching. Gymnaze de seauton pros eusebeian (train yourself for godliness) — gymnaō is athletic training: the discipline required for godliness is the same habituating, repeated effort as athletic conditioning.
4.8: hē gar sōmatikē gymnasia pros oligon estin ōphelimos — 'for physical training is of some value.' The concessive acknowledgment of physical training's value (pros oligon ōphelimos — useful for a little, to a limited degree) makes the comparison fair rather than dismissive. Hē de eusebeia pros panta ōphelimos estin (but godliness is beneficial for all things) — the contrast is scope: physical training benefits the present life only; godliness (eusebeia) benefits both present and future life (epangelian echousa zōēs tēs nun kai tēs mellousēs — holding promise of life both now and coming). The temporal contrast (now vs. the age to come) is the eschatological ground for prioritizing godliness.
4.9: pistos ho logos kai pasēs apodochēs axios — 'trustworthy is the saying and worthy of full acceptance.' The third occurrence of the pistos ho logos formula in 1 Timothy. The saying it endorses is either v.8 (godliness has promise for both lives) or v.10 (we labor because we hope in the living God) — the formula works in both directions. The repetition of the formula within one letter signals that Paul is marking recognized community affirmations, not merely his personal opinions.
4.10: eis touto gar kopiōmen kai agonizometha hoti ēlpikamen epi theō zōnti — 'for to this end we toil and strive, because we have set our hope on the living God.' Kopiōmen (we toil — present tense, ongoing) and agonizometha (we strive/agonize — the athletic contest verb) describe apostolic ministry as sustained effort requiring hope as its motivating foundation. Ēlpikamen (we have set our hope, perfect tense — with ongoing effect) — the settled nature of the hope is what makes sustained labor possible. Sōtēr pantōn anthrōpōn malista pistōn (Savior of all people, especially of those who believe) — the 'especially' (malista) distinguishes the mode of salvation: God is the Savior of all in some sense (providential preservation) but especially of those who believe in the soteriological sense.
4.11: paraggelle tauta kai didaske — 'command and teach these things.' The two verbs paraggelle (command, give orders — the military/authoritative term Paul uses for firm directives) and didaske (teach — the pedagogical term) pair authority with instruction: Timothy is to both assert the standard and explain it. The brevity of this imperative sentence after the extended theological argument (vv. 1-10) signals a return to direct address: the teaching must now be enacted.
4.12: mēdeis sou tēs neotētos kataphroneito — 'let no one despise your youth.' Kataphroneō (despise, look down on, treat with contempt) — the present imperative with negative forbids an ongoing pattern: opponents were already dismissing Timothy on grounds of youth. Neotēs (youth) — Timothy was probably in his 30s at the time, young for a significant leadership role by ancient standards where age carried authority. The counter-command: alla tupos ginou tōn pistōn (but become a pattern/example for the believers) — the word tupos (type, pattern, model) means a visible impression that others can imitate.
4.13: heōs erchomai prosece tē anagnōsei, tē paraklēsei, tē didaskaliā — 'until I come, devote yourself to the public reading of Scripture, to exhortation, to teaching.' The three activities form the synagogue-liturgy structure that early Christian worship inherited: anagnōsis (public reading of Scripture — the weekly Torah-and-Prophets reading), paraklēsis (exhortation — homiletical application), didaskalia (teaching — systematic doctrinal instruction). The construction 'until I come' makes these activities the regular ministry of Timothy's ongoing presence, not extraordinary measures.
4.14: mē amelei tou en soi charismatos ho edothē soi dia prophēteias meta epitheseōs tōn cheirōn tou presbyteriou — 'do not neglect the spiritual gift within you, which was given you through prophecy with the laying on of hands of the elders.' Amelei (neglect, be careless about) — the present imperative negative: Timothy is becoming negligent. The charism was given through two mechanisms: prophetic designation (dia prophēteias — a prophetic word identified Timothy as called) and the bodily act of the elders' hand-laying. The gift is therefore both divinely conferred and communally confirmed — not self-appointed or individually claimed.
4.15: tauta meleta, en toutois isthi hina sou hē prokopē phanera ē pasin — 'practice these things, be in them, so that your progress may be evident to all.' Meleta (practice, attend to, study) — the present imperative of ongoing discipline; en toutois isthi (be in these things) — total immersion in the ministry content. Prokopē (progress, advancement) — a Stoic term for moral and intellectual growth. The expectation that Timothy's growth will be phanera pasin (evident/visible to all) makes pastoral development a public, observable process — leadership formation happens in community view.
4.16: epimene autois; touto gar poiōn kai seauton sōseis kai tous akouontas sou — 'persist in them; for by doing this you will save both yourself and your hearers.' Epimene (persist, continue in) — the present tense of ongoing faithfulness. The final clause — sōseis kai seauton kai tous akouontas (you will save both yourself and your hearers) — is striking: Timothy's persistent faithfulness in doctrine and life is instrumental to salvation. This does not contradict justification by faith but asserts that the teacher's continuance in truth is the means through which ongoing saving knowledge reaches others.

5.1: presbyterō mē epiplēxēs alla parakalei hōs patera — 'do not rebuke an elder harshly, but encourage him as a father.' Epiplēssō (strike against, censure sharply — hapax legomenon in NT) is the harshest form of verbal reproof. Parakalei (encourage/exhort) — the pastoral verb that treats the person with dignity. The familial framework for community relationships: hōs patera (as a father), hōs adelphous (as brothers), hōs mēteras (as mothers), hōs adelphas (as sisters) — maps the community onto the extended family structure that was the primary social unit of the ancient world.
5.2: neōteras hōs adelphas en pasē hagneia — 'younger women as sisters, with absolute purity.' Hagneia (purity, chastity) — the qualifier for the sister-relationship: the familial model is invoked precisely to define appropriate limits, not to establish emotional closeness. The word appears in v.22 as well (seauton hagnon tērei — keep yourself pure) — Timothy's personal purity is the pastoral qualification for appropriate cross-gender relationships in ministry.
5.3: chēras tima tas ontōs chēras — 'honor widows who are truly widows.' Tima (honor) — the same word used in v.17 for honoring well-functioning elders, and in 6:1 for slaves honoring masters; it carries both relational respect and material provision (cf. Matt 15:5-6 where timē clearly means financial support). Ontōs chēras (really/truly widows) — the adverb distinguishes those genuinely without support from those with family to care for them. The passage defines 'true widow' across vv.3-16 in terms of age, marital history, piety, and absence of family.
5.4: ei de tis chēra tekna ē ekgona echei, manthanetōsan prōton ton idion oikon eusebeein — 'but if a widow has children or grandchildren, let them first learn to show godliness to their own household.' Manthanetōsan (let them learn) — the subjects are the children/grandchildren, not the widow. The verb 'learn' (manthanō) for fulfilling family obligations treats it as a matter of formation and practice: caring for elderly family members is not instinctive but learned and habituated. Amoimous apodidounai tois progonois (to make repayment to their forebears) — the language of reciprocal exchange: the parents who gave life and support are owed a return of care.
5.5: hē de ontōs chēra kai memonōmenē ēlpiken epi theon — 'the widow who is truly alone has set her hope on God.' Memonōmenē (having been left alone, perfect passive — with lasting result) characterizes her isolation not as a character trait but as her situation. Ēlpiken epi theon (has set her hope on God, perfect tense) — the settled orientation of her hope toward God is the spiritual mark of authentic widowhood. The continuous prayer (proseuchais kai tais deēsesin nuktos kai hēmeras — prayers and petitions night and day) echoes Anna's pattern in Luke 2:37 and reflects the hagiai (holy women) of early Christian ascetic practice.
5.6: hē de spatalōsa zōsa tethneiken — 'but the widow who lives for pleasure is dead even while she lives.' Spatalōsa (living luxuriously, self-indulgently — from spatalaō, to live wantonly, appear also in James 5:5) describes a life organized around pleasure-seeking. The paradox zōsa tethneiken (living she has died — perfect tense) echoes the spiritual death-in-life language of Eph 2:1 (you were dead in trespasses) and the contrast of 1 Tim 6:19 (laying up treasure for the age to come as 'truly life'). Self-indulgence is death even in the biological sense of life.
5.7: kai tauta paraggelle hina anenklētoi ōsin — 'command these things, so that they may be without blame.' The purpose clause hina anenklētoi ōsin (that they may be blameless/without accusation) extends to the widows and their families: the community's behavior toward widows and widows' own behavior must both pass scrutiny. The verb paraggelle (command) maintains the authoritative register: these are not suggestions but community-ordering directives.
5.8: ei de tis tōn idiōn kai malista oikeiōn ou pronoei, tēn pistin ērnētai — 'but if someone does not provide for their own, and especially for those of their household, they have denied the faith.' Pronoei (provide for, care for in advance) — the present tense of ongoing responsibility, not occasional charity. The severe judgment 'has denied the faith' (tēn pistin ērnētai — perfect tense, with ongoing effect) ranks family neglect with apostasy: the faith that saves produces care for dependents; faith without this care is a hollow claim. Apistou cheironos (worse than an unbeliever) — even pagans generally fulfilled family obligations from honor-culture expectations; the Christian who fails at this falls below the pagan baseline.
5.9: chēra katalegesthō mē elatton etōn hexēkonta gegoguia — 'let a widow be enrolled if she is not less than sixty years of age.' Katalegesthō (let her be enrolled/listed — the verb of official registration) implies a formal community roll (katalogos) of widows who receive regular support. The sixty-year minimum is a practical cut-off: younger widows could remarry and should (v.14). Henos andros gynē (wife of one husband — the feminine equivalent of 'husband of one wife' for overseers) — consistent marital fidelity is the criterion, not celibacy or widowhood at the time of enrollment.
5.10: en ergois kalois marturouménē — 'attested by good deeds.' The enrollment criterion includes a specific catalog of good works: ei eteknotrophēsen (if she raised children), ei exenodochēsen (if she showed hospitality — received strangers), ei hagiōn podas enipsen (if she washed the feet of the saints — a specific act of humble service, cf. John 13:1-17), ei thlibomenois epērkesen (if she helped the afflicted), ei panti ergō agathō epēkolouthēsen (if she devoted herself to every good work). The list itemizes hospitality, humble service, and mercy — the visible practices that constitute a 'good works' portfolio.
5.11: neōteras de chēras paraitou — 'but refuse younger widows.' Paraitou (refuse, decline — present middle imperative) is decisive: younger widows are not to be enrolled. The reason: hotan gar katastrēniasōsin tou Christou gamein thelousin (for when their strong desires overcome their dedication to Christ they want to marry). Katastrēniasōsin (to become wanton against, to feel strong sensual impulses overwhelming) — hapax legomenon in NT; a vivid verb for the physical desire overriding the consecrated commitment. The problem is not marriage itself (v.14 recommends it) but breaking a prior pledge to the community.
5.12: echouai krima hoti tēn prōtēn pistin ēthētēsan — 'having judgment because they have rejected their first pledge.' Ēthētēsan (have set aside, nullified — aorist of atheteō) — the deliberate nullification of a commitment previously made. Prōtēn pistin (first faith/pledge) — whether this means 'faith' (loyalty to Christ as a consecrated widow) or 'pledge' (a formal commitment made upon enrollment) is debated. Either way, the 'judgment' (krima) is the self-imposed consequence of their own breach of commitment, not external condemnation.
5.13: hama de kai argai manthanousin perierchomonai tas oikias — 'besides, they learn to be idle, going about from house to house.' Argai (idle, unoccupied — from a-ergos, without work) echoes the ataktoi (idle ones) of 2 Thess 3:11. Perierchomonai (going about, wandering around) suggests undirected movement. The compound vice: idle + wandering produces phluaroi (gossips, chatterers) and periergoI (busybodies — again the periergazomenoi wordplay of 2 Thess 3:11) who 'say things they ought not.' The result is community disruption through idle talk.
5.14: boulomaioun neōteras gamein, teknogonein, oikodespotein — 'I want younger widows to marry, to have children, to manage their households.' The three infinitives are deliberate: gamein (marry), teknogonein (bear children), oikodespotein (manage/master the household — a rare compound; the oikodespotēs was the household head with full management authority). Paul's counsel reverses the enrollment-restriction: the right response for younger widows is to channel energy into family formation, not enrolled celibacy. Mēdemian aphormēn didonai tō antikeimeno loidorias charin (giving the adversary no occasion for slander) — the practical motivation: vulnerable young widows in the community were targets for criticism from outsiders.
5.15: ēdē gar tines exetrapēsan opisō tou Satana — 'for some have already strayed after Satan.' Exetrapēsan (have turned aside, strayed — the same verb as in 1:6 for the false teachers turning to meaningless talk) marks the outcome Paul is trying to prevent: the deviation is already happening. 'After Satan' (opisō tou Satana) — the same adversarial figure who blocked Paul in 1 Thess 2:18 and fills the role of the one who tempted (1 Thess 3:5). Straying from community order into gossip and idleness is interpreted as moving toward the adversary's sphere.
5.16: ei tis pistē echei chēras, eparkeitō autais — 'if any believing woman has widows in her care, let her assist them.' The switch to the feminine pistē (believing woman) from the expected pistos (believing person) indicates a specific instruction to women of means: wealthy women in the community who had widows as household dependents are to care for them privately. The goal: mē bareisthō hē ekklēsia (that the church may not be burdened) — community resources are finite and should be reserved for widows with no other source of support.
5.17: hoi kalōs proestōtes presbyteroi diplēs timēs axiousthōsan — 'let elders who lead well be considered worthy of double honor.' Diplēs timēs (double honor) — the word timē covers both respect and material remuneration (cf. v.3 where it means support for widows). 'Double' may mean: honor in both respects (relational and material) or twice the normal rate of support. Malista hoi kopiōntes en logō kai didaskalia (especially those who labor in preaching and teaching) — the elder who teaches is worth more community support than the elder who leads without teaching, because the labor of exposition is the more demanding task.
5.18: legei gar hē graphē — 'for the Scripture says.' The dual citation that follows — boun aloōnta ou phimōseis (Deut 25:4) and axios ho ergatēs tou misthou autou (Luke 10:7 / Matt 10:10) — places a dominical saying on the same level as Torah by the shared verb legei hē graphē. This is one of the earliest explicit treatments of a saying of Jesus as 'Scripture' (graphē). The hermeneutical move: the agricultural law about oxen (Deut 25:4) is applied allegorically to human workers, as Paul does explicitly in 1 Cor 9:9-12, where he says 'it is written for our sake.'
5.19: kata presbyterou katēgorian mē paradechou — 'do not admit an accusation against an elder except on the basis of two or three witnesses.' The two-witness rule derives from Deut 19:15 (a charge must be established by two or three witnesses) — a foundational Jewish legal principle. Its application to elders provides both protection against false accusations (elders are visible and vulnerable targets) and due process: accusations require substantiation before Timothy acts on them. The principle protects institutional stability while not immunizing leaders from accountability.
5.20: tous hamartanontas enōpion pantōn elegche — 'those who persist in sin, rebuke in the presence of all.' Hamartanontas (sinning — present participle, ongoing sin) distinguishes this from a past resolved failure: the rebuke is for those continuing in sin after private address has failed. Enōpion pantōn (in the presence of all) — public rebuke preserves the community's moral seriousness and creates a deterrent effect: hina kai hoi loipoi phobon echōsin (that the rest may stand in fear). The public discipline serves the entire community, not just the individual being addressed.
5.21: diamartyromai enōpion tou theou kai Christou Iēsou kai tōn eklektōn angelōn — 'I charge you in the presence of God and of Christ Jesus and of the elect angels.' The tripartite witness — God, Christ, angels — frames what follows as a solemn obligation performed before the ultimate audience. Eklektōn angelōn (elect/chosen angels) — a distinctive phrase, contrasting with the fallen angels who serve the adversary; the chosen angels witness and ratify the divine administration of the church. Hina tauta phylassēs chōris prokrimatos mēden poiōn kata prosklēsin (that you keep these things without prejudging, doing nothing from favoritism) — partiality and pre-judgment are the twin corruption risks in disciplinary proceedings.
5.22: cheiras tacheōs mēdeni epitithei mēde koinōnei hamartiais allotriois — 'do not be hasty in the laying on of hands, and do not participate in the sins of others.' The warning against hasty hand-laying is connected to appointment and ordination: rapid appointment of someone not sufficiently tested creates vicarious responsibility for the appointee's subsequent sins. The second clause — mēde koinōnei hamartiais allotriois — extends this: complicity in others' sins through endorsement (including premature ordination) makes one partially responsible for those sins. Seauton hagnon tērei (keep yourself pure) — the positive counterpart: personal purity is the foundation of discerning appointment.
5.23: mēketi hydropótei, alla oinō oligō chrō — 'no longer drink only water, but use a little wine for your stomach and your frequent illnesses.' The brief personal medical advice breaks from the public-ministry instructions to address Timothy's physical condition. The pastoral interruption has often puzzled commentators: why here? Possibly Timothy's asceticism (drinking only water) was being maintained out of deference to the false teachers' prohibitions (4:3); Paul's counter is practical and pastoral. Pyknais sou astheneiais (your frequent illnesses) — Timothy evidently had chronic health difficulties that the water-only regime was not alleviating.
5.24: tinōn anthrōpōn hai hamartiai prodēloi eisin proagousai eis krisin — 'the sins of some people are conspicuous, leading them forward to judgment.' The verse illustrates the difficulty of discerning fitness for ordination (v.22): some people's sins are visible in advance of any formal assessment (prodēloi — obvious beforehand, manifest already); others' sins are hidden and will only emerge later. The two categories require different evaluative approaches: the obvious disqualifies immediately; the hidden will eventually be revealed even if not now. The same principle applies to good deeds (v.25): some are visible, some are not, but they too cannot remain permanently hidden.
5.25: hōsautōs kai ta erga ta kala prodēla — 'likewise good deeds are conspicuous.' The structural parallel with v.24 applies the same visibility-logic to positive character evidence: some good works are immediately visible, others are not. Kai ta allōs echonta krubēnai ou dunantai (and even things that are otherwise cannot remain hidden forever) — the eschatological undercurrent: full disclosure is inevitable in the divine economy. For appointment purposes, this means both that hidden sins will eventually be revealed (warranting caution in appointment) and that hidden virtues will emerge over time (warranting patience in evaluation).
